and those who might want, wish to be participants about
what some of the issues that they saw, um, in terms
of operating in the New Zealand market might be, uh,
what the barriers were
and might, what might facilitate their entry.
You know, what, what, what would it take
to get more entrance to the market?
Uh, had a number of, of, um, responses to that.
RFI, uh, we've
provided some analysis for that, uh,
to the responsible minister.
Uh, she is currently, uh, working through that,
thinking about what the options are in terms of, you know,
responding to those blockages, uh, contemporaneously, she's,
uh, I think publicly stated that she's asked us for,
to do some thinking around, uh,
what some structural options might be.
So she wants to have a full range of,
of levers available to her on the table.
Uh, no decisions have been taken at this stage. Yeah.
Um, I guess what I was trying to ask is, um,
that structural preparation piece, uh, granted
that you'll have a lot of information, um,
becoming available from potential entrants or competitors
or probably small businesses who are affected
by the monopsony power.
Um, but I'm asking about how you, how do you,
and how do we get an insight into the effect
of structural breakup when that is something that, um,
isn't dependent on, um, the sort of information
that would be provided by external stakeholders?
It's really, we are really dependent on, um,
government advice for that and the work that ComCom
Has done. Yeah. And we have
in the past done cost benefit assessments
of the range because there's more than one way
to, to think about structure.
Yeah. Uh, we've previously, uh,
for the previous government did some work on that.
Uh, we will be refreshing our thinking around that and,
and thinking, and without going into the detail, as I say,
there's, there's many ways to skin a cat in terms
of thinking about the things that you could do.
We'll provide advice, uh, to, to government as, as requested
around the range of those things, including estimates around
consumer and producer surplus, where that falls.
Okay. What the impacts of dynamic effects will be over time.
Uh, what the impacts of, uh,
relative productive efficiency might be versus the dynamic,
um, allocated efficiencies
that you might see from any of those changes. That's really
Helpful. Okay. One
more, we'll have to, we'll have to finish there.
Thank you so much. Thank.

source_metadata.description:
Thursday, 19 June 2025 | Economic Development, Science and Innovation Committee

2025/26 Estimates for Vote Business, Science and Innovation (excluding appropriations related to the Health Research Fund and New Zealand Trade and Enterprise)

Hearing of evidence:

Minister of Commerce and Consumer Affairs
	Hon Scott Simpson

Ministry of Business, Innovation, and Employment
	Paul Stocks, Deputy Secretary, Building, Resources and Markets
	Andrew Hume, General Manager Commerce, Consumer and Business
	Jenna Reid, Policy Director
